metadata.description: 基数排序: Bitwise sort from low to high (bit stable sort). O(d·n). Applicable for integers/fixed-length strings. Here is a brief Python code example using a bit-stable sorting algorithm, such as radix sort: ```python def bit_stable_sort(arr): def count_sort_for_radix(arr, exp): n = len(arr) output = [0] * n count = [0] * 10 # Store count of occurrences in count[] for i in range(n): index = (arr[i] // exp) % 10 count[index] += 1 # Change count[i] so that count[i] now contains actual position of this digit in output[] for i in range(1, 10): count[i] += count[i - 1] # Build the output array i = n - 1 while i >= 0: index = (arr[i] // exp) % 10 output[count[index] - 1] = arr[i] count[index] -= 1 i -= 1 # Copy the output array to arr[], so that arr[] now contains sorted numbers according to current digit for i in range(n): arr[i] = output[i] # Find the maximum number to know number of digits max1 = max(arr) # Do counting sort for every digit. Note that instead of passing digit number, exp is passed. exp is 10^i where i is current digit number exp = 1 while max1 // exp > 0: count_sort_for_radix(arr, exp) exp *= 10 # Example usage: arr = [170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66] bit_stable_sort(arr) print("Sorted array is:", arr) ``` This example demonstrates a bit-stable sorting algorithm (radix sort) that sorts integers based on individual digits from the least significant to the most significant.

data_pointers.title: 基数排序

Note that instead of passing digit number, exp is passed. exp is 10^i where i is current digit number exp = 1 while max1 // exp > 0: count_sort_for_radix(arr, exp) exp *= 10 # Example usage: arr = [170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66] bit_stable_sort(arr) print("Sorted array is:", arr) ``` This example demonstrates a bit-stable sorting algorithm (radix sort) that sorts integers based on individual digits from the least significant to the most significant.
